statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
Alice, Bob, and Carol visit a spring to collect water. Alice visits every $ a $ days (on days $ a, 2a, 3a, \dots $ ), Bob visits every $ b $ days (on days $ b, 2b, 3b, \dots $ ), and Carol visits every $ c $ days (on days $ c, 2c, 3c, \dots $ ).

When only one person visits, they collect $ 6 $ liters of water. If multiple people visit, the water is divided equally: two people take $ 3 $ liters each, and three people take $ 2 $ liters each.

Your task is to calculate how much water Alice, Bob, and Carol collect over $ m $ days, starting from day $ 1 $ .

Input Format
The first line contains a single integer $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ) — the number of test cases.

The only line of each test case contains four integers $ a $ , $ b $ , $ c $ , and $ m $ ( $ 1 \le a, b, c \le 10^6 $ ; $ 1 \le m \le 10^{17} $ ).

Output Format
For each test case, print three integers — the number of liters of water that Alice, Bob, and Carol collect over $ m $ days.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
4
2 1 3 10
1 1 8 5
6 20 15 1000
650650 1092 157437 100000000000000000
Output
14 38 8
15 15 0
881 236 281
845294870595 549337065358857 3774389867286

Note
Consider the first example:

- on day $ 1 $ , only Bob visits the spring, and he collects $ 6 $ liters;
- on day $ 2 $ , Alice and Bob visit the spring, and they each collect $ 3 $ liters;
- on day $ 3 $ , Bob and Carol visit the spring, and they each collect $ 3 $ liters;
- on day $ 4 $ , Alice and Bob visit the spring, and they each collect $ 3 $ liters;
- on day $ 5 $ , only Bob visits the spring, and he collects $ 6 $ liters;
- on day $ 6 $ , Alice, Bob, and Carol visit the spring, and they each collect $ 2 $ liters;
- on day $ 7 $ , only Bob visits the spring, and he collects $ 6 $ liters;
- on day $ 8 $ , Alice and Bob visit the spring, and they each collect $ 3 $ liters;
- on day $ 9 $ , Bob and Carol visit the spring, and they each collect $ 3 $ liters;
- on day $ 10 $ , Alice and Bob visit the spring, and they each collect $ 3 $ liters.

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2204C - Spring
Let's write a function $$$get(x, y, z, n)$$$ that calculates how many liters of water a person will collect over $$$n$$$ days if they come every $$$x$$$ days, while two other people come every $$$y$$$ and $$$z$$$ days. The answers to the problem will be $$$get(a, b, c, m)$$$, $$$get(b, a, c, m)$$$, and $$$get(c, a, b, m)$$$.
How do we calculate $$$get(x, y, z, n)$$$? Let's try to divide the days into three categories: those when one person comes, those when two people come, and those when all three come (we will assume that among the people who come, there is always the person who comes every $$$x$$$ days).
How do we count the number of days when only the first person comes? At first glance, it seems that there are $$$\lfloor \frac{n}{x} \rfloor$$$ of them. However, we will count the days when they come with someone else too; therefore, we need to subtract the number of days when the first and second person come, as well as the number of days when the first and third person come. But we have subtracted the days when all three come too many times, so we need to add that back.
The number of days when two people (including the first) come can be counted similarly: we take all the days when the first and second come, add all the days when the second and third come, and subtract twice the days when all three come (we counted these days twice, but we didn't need to count them at all).
Now we need to understand how to count the number of days when the first and second must come (and possibly the third). These are all days $$$i$$$ for which $$$i \bmod x = 0$$$ and $$$i \bmod y = 0$$$; in other words, $$$i \bmod LCM(x,y) = 0$$$, where $$$LCM(x, y)$$$ is the least common multiple of $$$x$$$ and $$$y$$$. Thus, there are exactly $$$\lfloor \frac{n}{LCM(x,y)} \rfloor$$$ such days. The number of days when the first and third must come (and possibly the second) is counted similarly.
Finally, we need to count the number of days when all three come. The number of such a day must be divisible by both $$$x$$$, $$$y$$$, and $$$z$$$; that is, it must be divisible by $$$LCM(x, y, z)$$$, and there will be $$$\lfloor \frac{n}{LCM(x,y,z)} \rfloor$$$ such days.
This is one possible implementation. It can be generalized to a larger number of people using the inclusion-exclusion principle, but that was not required in this problem.
